title: Create Methods In C# Console Applications - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of method syntax, parameters, return types, void methods, method overloading, out/ref parameters, optional parameters, and named arguments

questions.question: What does the `void` return type indicate in a C# method declaration?

options.text: The method returns an integer
The method returns null
The method does not return a value
The method returns an empty string

questions.explanation: The `void` keyword indicates that a method does not return any value. A void method performs actions but you cannot assign its result to a variable. Use the `return;` statement (without a value) to exit early from a void method.

questions.question: What is method overloading in C#?

options.text: Defining a method in a derived class that replaces a base class method
Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists
Calling the same method multiple times in a row
Creating a method that calls itself recursively

questions.explanation: Method overloading means having multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists (different number, types, or order of parameters). The compiler determines which overload to call based on the arguments passed.

questions.question: What does the `ref` keyword do when used with a method parameter?

options.text: Makes the parameter optional
Passes the argument by reference, so changes inside the method affect the original variable
Creates a copy of the argument
Prevents the parameter from being modified

questions.explanation: The `ref` keyword passes a variable by reference, meaning the method operates directly on the original variable. Any changes to the parameter inside the method are reflected in the caller's variable. The variable must be initialized before being passed.

questions.question: What is the difference between `ref` and `out` parameters in C#?

options.text: There is no difference
`ref` requires the variable to be initialized before passing; `out` does not
`out` requires initialization; `ref` does not
`ref` is for value types only; `out` is for reference types only

questions.explanation: `ref` requires the variable to be initialized before passing to the method. `out` does not require initialization before passing, but the method MUST assign a value to the `out` parameter before returning. Both pass by reference.

questions.question: How do you declare an optional parameter in a C# method?

options.text: By using the `optional` keyword
By assigning a default value in the parameter declaration
By wrapping the parameter in square brackets
By using the `?` operator after the parameter name

questions.explanation: Optional parameters are declared by providing a default value: `void Greet(string name, string greeting = "Hello")`. If the caller omits the argument, the default value is used. Optional parameters must come after all required parameters.

questions.question: What are named arguments in C#?

options.text: Arguments that must be passed by name instead of position
Arguments where you specify the parameter name followed by a colon and the value, allowing any order
Arguments that have been given a variable name
Arguments passed to named methods only

questions.explanation: Named arguments let you specify which parameter receives the value by using the format `parameterName: value`. This allows passing arguments in any order: `PrintInfo(age: 25, name: "Alice");`

questions.question: Which method signature is valid in C#?

options.text: int Add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
function Add(a, b) { return a + b; }
def Add(int a, int b): return a + b
Add(int a, int b) -> int { return a + b; }

questions.explanation: C# method syntax is: `returnType MethodName(parameterType parameterName, ...) { body }`. The return type comes first, followed by the method name and parameters with their types. Option B is JavaScript, C is Python, and D is Rust-like syntax.

questions.question: What happens if a non-void method doesn't include a `return` statement?

options.text: It returns null automatically
It returns the default value of the return type
The code will not compile (compile-time error)
It throws a runtime exception

questions.explanation: In C#, a non-void method must return a value on all code paths. If any path through the method doesn't return a value, the compiler generates an error: 'not all code paths return a value.'

options.text: Compile error

questions.explanation: The `Square` method returns the square of its parameter: 5 * 5 = 25. The return value (25) is then passed to `Console.WriteLine()`, which prints 25.

questions.question: Can two overloaded methods differ ONLY in their return type?

options.text: Yes, that's a valid overload
No, they must differ in their parameter list
Yes, but only if one returns void
Yes, but only for static methods

questions.explanation: Method overloading requires the methods to have different parameter lists (different number, types, or order of parameters). Two methods with the same name and parameter list but different return types will cause a compile error — the compiler can't determine which to call.
